title: Cuban mojo chicken
subtitle: Roasted chicken in citrus and garlic mojo marinade
excerpt: Chicken pieces marinated in Cuban mojo (garlic, orange juice, lime, cumin, and oregano), then roasted at high heat until the skin is deeply golden and the meat is fragrant with citrus. The same marinade as lechón asado, applied to a weeknight chicken.
sourceNotes: Cuban mojo criollo, the citrus-garlic marinade, is the defining flavour element of Cuban cooking. This recipe applies it to chicken pieces rather than a whole pork shoulder, making the same flavour profile accessible on a weeknight timescale. Sour orange (naranja agria) is the traditional citrus; equal parts orange juice and lime juice is the standard UK substitute. The recipe is a straightforward application of mojo technique to chicken thighs and drumsticks rather than a traditional dish with a specific name.

content.text: Mojo criollo, the Cuban citrus and garlic marinade, works as well on chicken as it does on pork. The acid in the orange juice and lime tenderises the meat and carries the garlic flavour into the flesh; the fat from the olive oil bastes the skin during roasting. The result is chicken that tastes unmistakably Cuban: bright with citrus, deep with garlic and cumin, with well-crisped skin.

Two hours of marinating is enough to taste the mojo in the finished chicken; overnight is better. The chicken goes into a hot oven skin-side up and is not turned, the skin needs sustained direct heat to crisp properly.

Blend or pound the {{garlic}} of garlic with the {{orange-juice}} of orange juice, the juice of the {{lime}} limes, {{cumin-ground}} of cumin, {{oregano-dried}} of oregano, {{olive-oil}} of olive oil, and {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt to a rough paste. Score the {{chicken-thigh}} chicken thighs and {{chicken-drumsticks}} drumsticks with a knife: two deep cuts per piece. Rub the mojo into the cuts and all over the surface. Refrigerate for at least 2 hours.

Preheat the oven to 200°C fan. Remove the chicken from the fridge 30 minutes before cooking. Arrange skin-side up in a roasting tin without crowding. Pour any remaining marinade around the pieces. Roast for 40 to 45 minutes until the skin is deep golden and the juices run clear when the thickest part of a thigh is pierced. Rest for 5 minutes before serving.

items.fix: Bring chicken to room temperature before roasting. Pat the top of the skin lightly to remove excess marinade before placing in the oven, marinade pooling on top of the skin prevents browning.
items.cause: Chicken too cold going in; marinade pooled on top of the skin
items.symptom: Skin pale and soft rather than golden and crisp

items.fix: Score the meat to the bone so the mojo penetrates. 2 hours gives a noticeable result; overnight is substantially better.
items.cause: Marinade time too short; cuts not deep enough
items.symptom: Mojo flavour faint in the finished chicken

items.fix: The orange juice should slightly outweigh the lime. If the pan juices taste sharply sour, add a small splash of water to the tin for the last 10 minutes of cooking to dilute them.
items.cause: Marinade over-reduced around the chicken during roasting; too much lime

items.fix: Keep the marinade blended so the garlic is distributed throughout. Loose minced garlic on a hot roasting tin will burn at 200°C.
items.cause: Loose garlic pieces on the tin surface at high heat
items.symptom: Garlic burning in the roasting tin

content.text: Mojo criollo is the flavour of Cuban cooking in the way that sofrito is the flavour of Puerto Rican cooking. It appears in some form in the majority of Cuban meat dishes. Applied to chicken, it is the weeknight version of what lechón asado does to a whole pork shoulder over a day. The sour orange that defines the original mojo is not commercially available in the UK; the orange-and-lime combination captures the same character. Serve with moros y cristianos, white rice and black beans, or tostones.
